title: Simple analytical flux-tuned iSWAP pulses for leakage suppression
authors: Dimitrios Georgiadis, Boxi Li, Asier Galicia, Rami Barends, F. A. Cárdenas-López, Felix Motzoi
abstract: Fast, high-fidelity two-qubit gates are a key requirement for fault-tolerant quantum computation. Tunable coupler architectures provide a flexible approach for implementing entangling gates through flux control with large on-off ratios, but fast flux modulation can induce diabatic transitions and population leakage to non-computational states, limiting gate performance. Here we present an analytical flux control method enabling derivative removal by adiabatic gate ($Φ$-DRAG) for suppressing leakage in flux tunable two-qubit gates. We show that $Φ$-DRAG differs fundamentally from conventional microwave implementations and derive modified flux modulation protocols that suppress leakage below $10^{-4}$ for fast entangling gates. The method remains effective across a range of asymmetry between qubit anharmonicities and different circuit parameters, enabling high-fidelity two-qubit gates within the fifteen nanosecond range.
summary: The paper presents an analytical method called $\Phi$-DRAG to suppress leakage errors during fast iSWAP gates implemented using tunable couplers in superconducting circuits. By applying higher-order derivative corrections to the flux modulation protocol, the authors achieve gate fidelities and leakage suppression below $10^{-4}$ even for rapid operations. This technique offers a robust pathway toward scaling up quantum computation by mitigating key hardware limitations.
main_problem: Fast, high-fidelity two-qubit gates are essential for quantum computation, but fast flux modulation in tunable coupler architectures causes diabatic transitions leading to population leakage.
main_result: The authors developed an analytical method ($\Phi$-DRAG) that suppresses leakage below $10^{-4}$ while enabling fast iSWAP gates within the fifteen nanosecond range.
method: They derived modified flux modulation protocols by applying higher-order derivative corrections directly to the effective interaction Hamiltonian, analogous to DRAG techniques used in microwave control.
model_or_system: The system involves superconducting qubits (transmons) coupled via a flux-tunable coupler. The dynamics are modeled using an effective Hamiltonian derived under the dispersive regime.
key_observables: Leakage probability to non-computational states (e.g., $|200angle$), gate fidelity, and the required coupling strength $g_{	ext{eff}}(t)$.
important_parameters: Gate time ($	au_f$), anharmonicity asymmetry ($\xi$), and the desired leakage suppression threshold ($< 10^{-4}$).
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on an effective Hamiltonian derived via Schrieffer-Wolff transformation, and higher-order corrections are limited by waveform distortions and AWG bandwidth.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the tunable coupler architecture, show time evolution plots comparing leakage suppression with $\Phi$-DRAG pulses versus uncorrected pulses, and display pulse shapes/Fourier spectra demonstrating spectral nulls.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem of leakage in fast flux-tunable gates, introduces the $\Phi$-DRAG method by deriving analytical corrections (second and fourth order) to the effective coupling, and validates these protocols against varying asymmetry and gate times.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, analytically derived pulse engineering technique ($\Phi$-DRAG) specifically tailored for superconducting flux-tunable qubits, which is highly relevant for researchers working on open quantum systems and high-fidelity control in solid-state platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Tunable-coupler architecture and operating principle. a) Circuit diagram of the tunable-coupling architecture consisting of three tunable transmon circuits. Each transmon is characterized by its capacitance Cℓand Josephson energies E(a) J,ℓand E(b) J,ℓalong a loop threaded by an external magnetic flux φx,ℓ. The devices are pair-wise coupled through capacitances Ck,j. Blue represents Qb1 and Qb2, whereas the red one acts as the coupler. Each device is described by the node variable φℓ. b) Schematic of the working principle: by modulating the coupler flux, the effective qubit-qubit coupling geff is tuned from an idling point (where geff ≈0) to a value activating an iSWAP gate. c)...

FIG. 2. Population transfer fringe patterns. a) Population trans- fer |⟨100|U(t)|001⟩|2 as a function of time t and external coupler flux φext/φ0. Chevron-like fringes indicate the iSWAP resonance condi- tion, with faster swapping achievable at larger flux amplitudes. The green dashed line marks the idling point where geff ≈0. b) Corre- sponding leakage to the non-computational state, |⟨101|U(t)|200⟩|2, on a logarithmic scale. As the flux amplitude increases (shorter gate times), leakage to |200⟩grows rapidly and becomes the dominant source of coherent error.

FIG. 3. Performance of Φ-DRAG for the symmetric system (α1 = α2). a) Leakage probability to |200⟩(starting from |101⟩) at the end of an iSWAP gate as a function of gate time τ f , comparing cosine-like pulses, Gaussian-like pulses Eq. (10), and their Φ-DRAG2-corrected counterparts Eq. (8). Each data point corresponds to an iSWAP pulse whose amplitude has been optimized to satisfy the area condition Eq. (3). For the Gaussian-like pulses the rising time has been selected to ensure leakage suppression below the desired threshold 10−4 (dashed line). b) Effective coupling |geff(t)|/2π as a function of normalized time t/τf for Gaussian-like pulses at four gate times τf ∈{15, 20, 25, 30} ns. c)...

FIG. 4. Gate-time selectivity via rising time optimization for the Φ-DRAG2 correction. Leakage to |200⟩(starting from |101⟩) as a function of gate time τ f and pulse rising time τr for Gaussian-like pulses Eq. (10). a) Without Φ-DRAG correction: the leakage land- scape shows oscillatory minima as a function of both τ f and τr, that require picosecond precision to achieve. b) With Φ-DRAG2 correc- tion Eq. (8): joint optimization of rising time and gate time enables leakage below 10−4 across a broad parameter range, and the suppres- sion minima can be steered to any desired gate time by adjusting τr. Each point represents an area-optimized iSWAP pulse satisfying Eq. (3).

FIG. 5. Pulse shapes and Fourier spectra for second- and fourth-order Φ-DRAG corrections. a) Effective coupling geff(t)/2π as a function of time (left column) and its Fourier spectrum |˜g(ω)|/2π (right col- umn), shown for both the bare (initial) corresponding to Eq. (10), Φ-DRAG2, and Φ-DRAG4 waveforms. a,b) τ f = 20 ns gate: the Φ-DRAG2 correction creates spectral nulls at ±α1/2π (the transi- tion frequency driving leakage), while Φ-DRAG4 additionally ad- dresses the asymmetric case with nulls at ±α1/2π and ±α2/2π. c,d) τf = 15 ns gate: the broader spectral content required for faster gates leads to more complex corrected waveforms, making the Φ-DRAG4 pulse more susceptible to AWG...
